question_text: URGENT SECURITY + ACCESS INCIDENT: Since yesterday afternoon, multiple things broke right after a suspected phishing event. 1) Several employees opened what looked like normal PDF downloads, but then they were redirected to a very convincing Microsoft-style login page. Security now assumes credentials may have been harvested. 2) As an emergency reaction, IT tried to force all non-MFA logins through a country restriction, but now legitimate users traveling are blocked and business-critical work is stopped. 3) At the same time, a few critical automated accounts (backup and an integration service user) started failing to sign in and scheduled jobs broke, but we’re afraid to loosen the security controls. 4) And one internal integration suddenly cannot authenticate anymore even though the same account still works in the browser UI — the automation fails only in the background connection. People are blaming passwords and laptops, but this all started together. What is going on, and please confirm — should this be classified as a 1st-level or 2nd-level support ticket?
answer: Summary - what the indexed records explicitly document - A phishing vector was found where two PDF files contained links to a fake Microsoft login page that exfiltrated credentials to algina[.]co before redirecting to Microsoft - the likely purpose being credential harvesting [Data: Sources (4)]. - The customer asked to route all users without MFA through a geolocation control (target countries: CH, DE, AT, IT) and listed exceptions (several service accounts and D365IntPROD) and asked that Microsoft service‑initiated sign-ins not be blocked [Data: Sources (4)]. - Prior incidents in the records show that policy/configuration changes that affect authentication flows can break automation and integrations: - ADFS client parameters (EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders) set to true caused background authentication failures; adjusting those parameters to the TEST environment settings resolved the issue [Documented action in TI-1385257] [Data: Sources (11)]. - Enabling tenant Security Defaults caused SMTP AUTH to be disabled and legacy Outlook/automated senders to fail; selective re‑enabling of SMTP AUTH and Conditional Access adjustments restored mail flow [Documented action in TI-1548823] [Data: Sources (32)]. - A reverse proxy / WAF reload after a security patch removed a required header (X-Forwarded-Proto) and caused external logins to fail; restoring the custom HTTP profile/header rewrite per the architecture blueprint resolved access [Documented action in TI-1594408] [Data: Sources (15)]. - The KF12 ticket was classified as Priority HIGH and the notes propose a Lessons‑Learned meeting and SOC/ModernSecOps topics, indicating this situation was treated as a significant security incident [Data: Sources (4)]. Interpreting your four symptoms from the indexed records - Credential harvesting via a convincing fake login (item 1) is documented in KF12 and should be treated as a possible credential compromise event [Data: Sources (4)]. - The emergency reaction to force non‑MFA logins through geolocation controls (item 2) is the exact control requested in KF12 but the record does not document the final applied configuration or outcomes - however, the other tickets show that such policy/configuration changes commonly cause legitimate breakage when applied broadly (examples: TLS inspection missing headers, Security Defaults disabling legacy auth, ADFS param changes) [Data: Sources (4, 15, 32, 11)]. - Automated accounts and scheduled jobs failing (item 3) match prior incidents where policy changes (Security Defaults / Conditional Access or SMTP AUTH) prevented non‑interactive/legacy authentication; the Exchange case shows selective enabling of SMTP AUTH and Conditional Access adjustments fixed that class of failure [Data: Sources (32)]. - Integrations that work in browser UI but fail in background (item 4) match the ADFS/JWT/MFA misconfiguration incident where interactive browser logins succeeded but background/service authentication failed due to token/MFA delegation settings; adjusting the ADFS client parameters resolved it [Data: Sources (11)]. Ticket classification recommendation (documented evidence + reasoning) - The indexed records show: (a) confirmed credential‑harvesting phishing activity (TI‑858473 / KF12), (b) this class of incident was treated as HIGH priority and discussed as requiring lessons learned and SOC topics (TI‑858473), and (c) resolving related authentication breaks required system‑engineer / specialist escalation in other incidents (TI‑1385257, TI‑1548823, TI‑1594408). On that basis, this should be treated as at least a 2nd‑level / security‑incident ticket (escalate to Security/SOC and System Engineers), not a pure 1st‑level support call [Data: Sources (4, 11, 32, 15)]. - This is a recommendation based on how similar incidents were handled in the indexed records (escalations and specialist actions). The records do not contain a formal "1st vs 2nd level" policy mapping for this exact scenario; the above is an interpretation supported by the examples cited [Data: Sources (4, 11, 32, 15)]. Step‑by‑step resolution plan - actions documented in the records (map to this case) (Each step below cites the ticket(s) where that action or finding was actually performed.) 1) Treat as a security incident and escalate to Security/SOC and System Engineering - Rationale / precedent: KF12 was priority HIGH and marked for Lessons‑Learned/SOC topics; other authentication incidents were forwarded to System Engineers (Exchange Specialist, ADFS specialist) for fixes [Data: Sources (4, 11, 32, 15)]. 2) Triage & evidence gathering (documented methods used in prior tickets) - Collect sign‑in logs / Azure AD sign‑in data for the suspected accounts and affected users to determine unusual locations/IPs and confirm which accounts used the fake page [Analogous method used in SharePoint/Sign‑in analysis] [Data: Sources (30)]. - For Exchange/SMTP failures, review Exchange mailflow queues and server event logs for 535/SMTP AUTH failures (this is what was done in TI‑1548823) [Data: Sources (32)]. - For integration/background failures, review ADFS / client registration settings and application logs to identify token errors or MFA delegation problems (as in TI‑1385257) [Data: Sources (11)]. 3) Contain likely‑compromised accounts (recommended action informed by records, but not fully specified there) - The KF12 record documents credential harvesting but does not list containment steps performed. In similar high‑risk cases in the corpus, escalation and targeted policy adjustments were used (e.g., policy changes in TI‑1548823 and TI‑1385257). The record does not show a documented password reset or global block for KF12, so the indexed knowledge is insufficient to state a specific containment action was taken in KF12 [Data: Sources (4, 32, 11)]. - Recommended (not directly documented): Immediately disable or enforce MFA/reset passwords for confirmed compromised accounts and monitor sign‑in logs. (Marking explicitly as recommended - see next section.) 4) Restore business‑critical automated accounts while maintaining security - follow documented fixes that match your symptoms - If mail/automation fails due to tenant policy changes disabling legacy auth (Security Defaults / Conditional Access), apply the documented fix: selectively re‑enable SMTP AUTH for the affected accounts and adjust Conditional Access policies so automation can continue while minimizing exposure [Documented remediation in TI‑1548823] [Data: Sources (32)]. - If an integration/authentication fails in background but interactive browser login works, check ADFS client registration for EnableJWT and RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders. In TI‑1385257 these parameters were incorrectly set to true and adjusting them to match TEST environment fixed the problem - replicate that check for the failing integration's ADFS client [Documented remediation in TI‑1385257] [Data: Sources (11)]. 5) Review and adjust geolocation / MFA routing rule to avoid legitimate lockouts - The KF12 ticket requested routing non‑MFA logins through geolocation and explicit exceptions (service accounts). The record does not show the exact firewall/WAF implementation or whether it was applied. Other tickets show that policy/template reloads after patches can overwrite exceptions and break access (TI‑1594408). Therefore: - Documented precedent: Compare current reverse proxy / WAF / firewall configuration to the architectural blueprint and reapply custom header/profile or exceptions as documented; in the portal case, restoring the custom header rewrite and profile restored external access [Data: Sources (15)]. - Ensure you implement the geolocation/MFA control only for interactive sign‑ins and explicitly exempt machine/service accounts (the KF12 request included such exceptions) [Data: Sources (4)]. - The indexed records do not contain the exact WAF/firewall rules you applied here, so you must verify which template or profile was overwritten and reapply the documented blueprint settings if relevant (as in TI‑1594408) [Data: Sources (15, 4)]. 6) Validate and monitor - After changes, monitor sign‑ins and mailflow for 24+ hours as done in TI‑1548823 and TI‑1594408; get customer confirmation that business processes work again and close ticket only after monitoring is stable [Data: Sources (32, 15)]. Where the indexed knowledge is insufficient - The KF12 record documents the phishing vector and the customer's requested mitigation but does not contain a complete, documented remediation flow (no universal containment steps, no record of password resets, no final geolocation rule implementation) - so the indexed knowledge alone is insufficient to fully resolve this incident end‑to‑end for your environment. The records provide close analogues (ADFS param fix, SMTP AUTH/Conditional Access adjustments, WAF header/profile restore) that map to your symptoms, but they do not provide the exact commands, policy names, or the final applied firewall/WAF rule set for your tenant [Data: Sources (4, 11, 32, 15)]. - Therefore, you will need to: collect current logs and configuration (ADFS client settings, Conditional Access policies, WAF/firewall templates and header rewrite rules, Azure AD sign‑in logs, Exchange send connector/auth settings) and escalate to Security/SysEng to take the concrete remediation steps listed above. The indexed tickets show those are the relevant components to check and the successful prior remediations for similar root causes [Data: Sources (11, 32, 15, 30)]. Concise operational checklist (which steps are documented in prior tickets vs which are recommended) - Documented in records and to be performed now where matching symptom exists: 1. Check ADFS client parameters (EnableJWT, RequestMFAFromClaimsProviders) for the failing integration - fix if set incorrectly (documented resolution in TI‑1385257) [Data: Sources (11)]. 2. Check whether tenant Security Defaults / Conditional Access disabled SMTP AUTH and, if so, selectively re‑enable SMTP AUTH for affected automation or adjust policies (documented resolution in TI‑1548823) [Data: Sources (32)]. 3. Review reverse proxy/WAF templates and header rewrite rules if external access is broken after a patch - reapply blueprint profile and header forwarding as done in TI‑1594408 (X‑Forwarded‑Proto example) [Data: Sources (15)]. 4. Review Azure AD sign‑in logs to identify suspicious sign‑ins and which accounts may be compromised (approach used in TI‑1578891/SharePoint analysis) [Data: Sources (30)]. - Recommended actions not explicitly performed in the KF12 record (you must treat these as steps to execute now): - Immediate containment for confirmed compromised credentials (disable or reset, enforce MFA). - Escalate to SOC / Incident Response team for forensic analysis and disclosure obligations. - Apply the geolocation/MFA control only after validating exceptions and testing effect on service accounts and traveling users. If you want, I can: - Produce a prioritized action list you can hand to the SOC and System Engineers (marking which items are directly supported by the records and which are recommended), or - Draft the exact investigative questions and logs to collect (sign‑in log queries, event log entries to retrieve, Conditional Access policy names to check) - note: the indexed records do not contain the specific queries, so those will be a recommended next step rather than a quotation of prior tickets. Which of those would you like me to prepare next?

question_text: Hi Helpdesk… sorry for the chaos but we’re really blocked and it’s all happening at the same time. In SharePoint, the project area for the 2025 migration is basically ‘walled off’ now. The site itself loads, other project spaces open, but THIS one folder/path just throws that classic “you don’t have rights / not allowed” message for a bunch of us who were working in it literally yesterday. Separately (maybe related?), Compliance did some housekeeping: they renamed a folder and slapped one of those ‘Confidential’ / lock-type classifications on it (you know the banner at the top with the label). Since then, access is totally inconsistent. Some people can open files, others get blocked, and one colleague claims it only works if he opens it in a private/incognito browser window. Same user, same link, different result depending on how he opens it… super weird. Also our external auditor can’t deliver their nightly package to our ‘secure drop’ anymore. From their side it just sits there and then times out. On our side it *looks* like the server exists and is up. We even tried WinSCP to reproduce and it behaves the same (hang → fail). They swear they didn’t change anything. While trying to coordinate all this in Teams, one key person’s webcam basically vanished. In Teams settings there’s no camera option at all (not even a dropdown), and even Teams in the browser can’t see a camera. But Windows still lists a camera device in Device Manager, so it doesn’t feel like the hardware is dead. And during the same Teams call, another person can hear everyone perfectly, but nobody can hear them. Teams shows a mic selected, but the little input bar never moves (like it’s stuck at zero). They’re on a USB headset. We already rebooted, swapped USB ports, tried a different browser, and did the usual ‘turn it off and on again’ stuff. Is this connected or just a cursed Monday? And please confirm: should this be classified as a 1st-level or 2nd-level support ticket?
answer: Summary of what the indexed records explicitly document (symptoms, root causes, diagnostic steps and fixes found useful) - A SharePoint folder that was renamed and then labelled with a sensitivity / encryption label caused inconsistent access for some users. In one prior case the label required encryption and limited access to a different Azure AD group than the one actually used for SharePoint permissions; adding a user into the label's allowed group restored access immediately (documented test and hypothesis) [Data: Sources (30)]. - In other SharePoint incidents the root cause for "some users can't see a folder, others can" was either (a) duplicate groups / On‑Prem vs Cloud group sync overwriting cloud memberships (external users lost access) [Data: Sources (13)], or (b) cached authentication tokens and Conditional Access evaluation delays (access restored after sign‑out / CA policy adjustment) [Data: Sources (38)]. - Camera-not-present in Teams was resolved where Windows privacy/settings had blocked camera access for apps; unlocking camera access for apps and enabling Teams restored camera availability (helpdesk performed OS privacy checks and adjusted permissions per a Blueprint) [Data: Sources (42)]. - Microphone no input on Teams with USB headset was resolved where the headset microphone plug was not fully connected; plugging it in correctly immediately restored the input level and call audio [Data: Sources (37)]. Step‑by‑step resolution plan based on documented diagnostic steps and fixes I will separate steps that are explicit records of actions that worked in prior tickets from recommended tests/actions that are not fully documented in these records. A. SharePoint / "walled off" project folder (documented steps you should run first; these were used in previous tickets) 1) Verify folder permission model and group referenced by SharePoint. Confirm whether the folder has unique permissions and which Azure AD group(s) are listed as having access. (This check was performed in TI-1578891 and TI-1571046 and led to further analysis) [Data: Sources (30, 38)]. 2) Review any Sensitivity / AIP label applied to that folder and its protection settings (who is allowed by the label). In the documented incident the label required encryption and restricted access to an Azure AD group different from the SharePoint permission group, which produced a conflict [Data: Sources (30)]. 3) Test access by adding a representative affected user temporarily to the Azure AD group that the label permits (documented test: adding the user to "Compliance_Core" immediately allowed access) - do this as a short, reversible test to confirm the label-enforcement path is the block [Data: Sources (30)]. 4) Check Azure AD Sign‑in / Token claims for affected users to see which group claims are present in the token and whether the label's required group appears. Prior work showed tokens for affected users contained the project group but not the label group; this is diagnostic evidence that label enforcement can block access [Data: Sources (30)]. 5) If behavior is inconsistent (works in Incognito but not regular session), have affected users fully sign out of all Office apps and browsers and sign in again (token cache / stale claims). In a prior case, clearing cached tokens and re‑authenticating restored access for users whose tokens were stale; where CA evaluation delay was a factor, reauthentication also helped after CA policy adjustments [Data: Sources (38, 30)]. B. External user / secure drop / WinSCP timeout (what the records show and limits) - What the records document: external users can lose effective access when two groups with identical names exist (one Cloud, one synchronized On‑Prem). SharePoint evaluates group membership by objectId; synchronized On‑Prem groups can overwrite cloud groups and drop external guests that were only in the cloud group. Consolidation and full Azure AD Connect sync fixed that scenario and restored external access in the documented incident [Data: Sources (13)]. - What the records do NOT document: specifics of WinSCP/SFTP timeouts to your "secure drop" are not described in the provided records. There is no documented WinSCP/SFTP diagnostic run or fix in the indexed tickets. Therefore I cannot assert a proven fix for the WinSCP timeout from the indexed knowledge. Explicitly: the indexed records do not contain enough information to fully resolve the SFTP/WinSCP transfer timeout issue. C. Teams camera missing and microphone no input (documented, common fixes) 1) Camera not visible in Teams (documented resolution): check Windows OS privacy settings and app permissions; enable camera access for apps and specifically allow Teams. In the documented case adjusting OS privacy settings and enabling Teams allowed the camera to appear and work again [Data: Sources (42)]. 2) Microphone selected but input level stuck at zero with USB headset (documented resolution): confirm physical headset connection - in the documented incident the mic cable on the headset was not fully plugged in. Reconnecting the mic properly immediately produced input signal and restored calls [Data: Sources (37)]. Which of the above should be escalated (1st vs 2nd level) - documented guidance - SharePoint issues that are clearly permission/group/label related and require analysis of AIP label enforcement, Azure AD group mappings, Azure AD Connect sync, or Conditional Access policy changes were escalated to BU Infrastructure - Identity / M365 in the documented cases and treated as 2nd‑level for in‑depth analysis (see TI-1578891 escalation and TI-1583327 full sync / group consolidation) [Data: Sources (30, 13)]. - Camera/mic issues were resolved by first‑level helpdesk performing OS privacy checks, app permission changes and simple device checks (Blueprint) or physically reconnecting a headset - these match 1st‑level resolution steps in the records [Data: Sources (42, 37)]. Concrete recommended next actions for your ticket (marked where these go beyond strictly documented fixes) (These recommendations largely follow the documented troubleshooting order above; where a prior ticket documents the exact action I cite it. Where the indexed records do not state the exact next action for your exact environment (for example, WinSCP protocol tracing), I mark it explicitly as recommended.) 1) Immediately collect evidence (documented best practice across tickets - helpdesk questions and tests): list affected user accounts (internal and external), times when access worked/didn't, one reproducible username that currently cannot access, the exact SharePoint path, and screenshots of the "Item not found / Access denied" messages. (This mirrors the initial information gathering used in TI-1578891 and others) [Data: Sources (30, 13)]. 2) Helpdesk (1st‑level) run these documented checks now: - Confirm the folder's SharePoint permission model (unique permissions?) and the Azure AD group(s) listed in the folder permissions [Documented: TI-1578891/TI-1571046] [Data: Sources (30, 38)]. - Check the applied Sensitivity/AIP label and its allowed group(s) (documented cause in TI-1578891) [Data: Sources (30)]. - Ask an affected user to test Incognito/private browsing and then to fully sign out of Office apps and sign back in; record whether that temporarily resolves it (documented symptomatic behaviour in TI-1578891 and TI-1571046) [Data: Sources (30, 38)]. 3) If step 2 shows a label->group mismatch (documented fix): as a diagnostic test temporarily add one affected user to the label's allowed Azure AD group (follow your org change management) to confirm label enforcement is the blocker - prior ticket documented that adding user to "Compliance_Core" immediately allowed access [Documented test: TI-1578891] [Data: Sources (30)]. If that proves it, plan remediation: align SharePoint permission group and label allowed group (documented corrective idea in TI-1578891). (RECOMMENDED: perform only with appropriate approvals - this next action is not prescriptive in the indexed records beyond the test that was performed). 4) If externals are affected (documented root cause for externals): check whether there are two groups with the same name (Cloud‑native and On‑Prem synchronized) and whether Azure AD Connect is overwriting the cloud group. The documented resolution was consolidation of groups, ensuring external guests are managed cloud‑only where appropriate, cleaning the On‑Prem group, and performing a full Azure AD Connect synchronization [Documented fix: TI-1583327] [Data: Sources (13)]. 5) If access is intermittent or works only after sign‑out: review Sign‑In logs and token claims to confirm whether the token contains required group claims (documented in TI-1578891/TI-1571046). If tokens are stale and Conditional Access is involved, reauthentication plus a temporary CA policy adjustment resolved the documented CA‑related case [Documented: TI-1571046] [Data: Sources (38)]. 6) For the external auditor WinSCP/SFTP timeout: the indexed records do not contain a documented resolution for SFTP/WinSCP transport timeouts. Recommended diagnostic actions (not documented in these records) include testing the transfer from an internal host, capturing network/packet or SFTP client logs, and verifying the destination's authentication and firewall/transport settings. Marking this as recommended because the indexed tickets do not provide a WinSCP resolution trace or playbook. 7) Teams camera and microphone - immediate 1st‑level steps you can run now (these are documented fixes): - Camera: Check Windows Privacy > Camera and ensure "Allow apps to access your camera" and Teams is allowed; after changing, restart Teams. The documented case used these Blueprint checks and restored camera availability [Documented: TI-1615042] [Data: Sources (42)]. - Microphone: Physically verify headset connections. In the documented case reconnecting the mic plug fixed the zero input level immediately. Also verify Windows Sound shows an input signal and test a Teams test call [Documented: TI-1616128] [Data: Sources (37)]. 8) If camera/mic checks do not resolve the Teams device issues, escalate to 2nd‑level (or the team that manages endpoints/driver policies) - in the camera case the issue was Windows privacy settings (helpdesk resolved), so escalate only if device drivers or blocked device policies appear involved (this escalation path is implied by the documented helpdesk->Blueprint steps) [Data: Sources (42)]. Ticket priority / level classification (based on prior handling in records) - Treat the SharePoint access problem (multiple internal users + external auditor unable to deliver files) as a 2nd‑level / Identity escalation if the initial helpdesk checks (folder permissions, label check, sign‑out/re‑auth) do not immediately resolve it. In the indexed incidents similar multi‑user / group / sync / label problems were escalated to BU Infrastructure - M365 / Identity for in‑depth analysis (TI-1578891, TI-1583327) [Data: Sources (30, 13)]. - Treat the Teams camera and the USB headset microphone issues as 1st‑level tasks: these were resolved by helpdesk using OS privacy/app permission checks and a device physical check in the documented tickets (TI-1615042 and TI-1616128) [Data: Sources (42, 37)]. If you want, I can now: - Produce an exact short checklist for your helpdesk to run immediately (based on the documented sequences above) - I will mark any items in that checklist that are recommended (not strictly recorded) vs those that are directly lifted from prior tickets. - Draft the escalation note to Identity to open a 2nd‑level ticket (including the specific facts to include: which folder, which label, sample affected user, sign‑in log observations). Final note on limits of the indexed knowledge - The records document fixes for label vs permission conflicts, group duplication via Azure AD Connect, and token/Conditional Access caching issues - all plausible matches for your symptom set and with concrete remediation steps already used in other tickets [Data: Sources (30, 13, 38)]. - However, the WinSCP/SFTP transfer timeout has no specific resolution in the indexed records; I cannot claim a documented fix for that particular symptom from the provided tickets. You will need to gather SFTP client logs/network traces and either (a) test SharePoint/destination reachability from an internal host or (b) involve your network or file transfer team (recommended next steps) - this recommendation is not a concrete documented fix within the indexed knowledge. Which documents support which steps (quick reference) - Sensitivity label conflict and test of adding user to label group: TI-1578891 [Data: Sources (30)]. - Token caching / Conditional Access evaluation delay and CA policy adjustment: TI-1571046 [Data: Sources (38)]. - Duplicate Cloud vs On‑Prem group and external users losing access; consolidation and full sync remediation: TI-1583327 [Data: Sources (13)]. - Camera missing because OS privacy/app permissions blocked Teams: TI-1615042 [Data: Sources (42)]. - Microphone no input due to headset cable not fully plugged in: TI-1616128 [Data: Sources (37)]. If you want, tell me which of the above you want executed first and I will produce the short actionable checklist (marking each item as "documented in records" vs "recommended").

question_text:
Hi IT-Team, sorry but we have a very odd combination of issues since yesterday and multiple teams are blocked.

First: In D365 (Finance & Operations), Financial Reports is basically unusable. When we go to General Ledger -> Financial Reports -> Edit, it either errors or just spins forever. It is not only one person: several users report it. Also, older reports suddenly show the author as "unknown", and when we try to open or create a report it sometimes says the report cannot be found. This is happening in test and production, so we cannot even use test as a workaround.

Second: our new hotline numbers from the current marketing campaign are dead. Calls to the old main number still ring through, but if a customer calls one of the new ones it fails right away. The carrier insists the calls reach our edge, but they never seem to land with anyone here.

Third: the Basel office has a very specific behavior on outside calls. The call connects, people can talk briefly, and then it cuts off after roughly half a minute almost every time, like clockwork. Internal calls are fine, and colleagues at other sites are fine. Apparently something on our voice setup was changed last week.

Fourth: a few team leads say they no longer get any kind of pop-up or alert when Teams messages come in on their laptops. Messages still arrive — they just only notice them once they actually open Teams. Their phones are unaffected. Windows updates ran last week and at least one person had some focus mode turned on during a meeting.

Is this connected or just multiple incidents at once? And please confirm: should this be classified as a 1st-level or 2nd-level support ticket?
answer: Summary - The indexed incident records show four separate, documented root causes and fixes for issues that match each of your four symptoms. The problems were treated independently in prior tickets; nothing in the records indicates a single common root cause tying all four together. The documented resolution steps and ownership differ per symptom. The relevant source tickets are cited per step below [Data: Sources (0, 1, 8, 16, 19)]. Per-issue, step-by-step resolutions (what the records explicitly document) 1) D365 Finance & Operations - Financial Reports: errors / "unknown" authors / "report cannot be found" Documented symptoms and diagnostics: - Issue reproducible for multiple users and in multiple environments; network and ADFS initially excluded; Report Designer sometimes extremely slow on AOS servers; AOS nodes showed different responsiveness. A Data Mart Reset and FRDB recreation were attempted; a Microsoft support ticket was opened when initial measures did not resolve it [Data: Sources (1, 0)]. Documented remediation steps that led to closure: - Best-practice cipher suites were activated using IIS Crypto and AOS servers were restarted. After those changes the ticket was closed as resolved [Data: Sources (0)]. Concrete steps based on the record (these steps are documented as actually performed in the prior case): - Recreate FRDB / perform Data Mart Reset (documented attempt) [Data: Sources (1)]. - If issue persists, open a Microsoft support ticket (documented) [Data: Sources (1)]. - Activate best-practice cipher suites (performed with IISCRYPTO) and restart AOS servers (documented closure action) [Data: Sources (0)]. 2) New hotline numbers not reaching users (carrier says calls reach edge) Documented symptoms and diagnostics: - Carrier reports INVITEs reach the edge/SBC but calls are not forwarded internally; SBC logs showed INVITE received but immediately answered with "404 Not Found" for affected number ranges. Investigation found the new number range was not included in the SBC translation table / routing rules [Data: Sources (8)]. Documented remediation steps that led to closure in the prior case: - Extend/adjust routing and translation rules on the SBC to include the new number range; test calls confirmed correct signalling and delivery after the temporary rule and final adjustment [Data: Sources (8)]. Concrete steps based on the record: - Check SBC signalling logs for the affected number range (look for INVITE/404 behaviour) [Data: Sources (8)]. - Verify translation table and routing rules on the SBC include the new number range; if missing, add/extend the translation rules accordingly [Data: Sources (8)]. - Test calls; revert/adjust as needed and then apply final routing rule changes per voice architecture [Data: Sources (8)]. 3) Basel office - external calls drop after ~30 seconds Documented symptoms and diagnostics: - External calls disconnect after ~29-31 seconds; SBC logs show BYE after ~30s. Investigation found Session-Expires header/session timer set to 30s on SBC and Basel's new Voice VLAN used a NAT policy with UDP session timeout of 30s (deviation vs other sites where timeout was 180s) [Data: Sources (16)]. Documented remediation steps that led to closure in the prior case: - Temporarily adjusted session parameters on the SBC to validate the hypothesis (calls stayed stable). Then coordinated with network team and changed the NAT session timeout and SBC/session parameters to match architecture specification; tests confirmed stability afterwards [Data: Sources (16)]. Concrete steps based on the record: - Review SBC call logs and Session-Expires/session timers (documented diagnostic) [Data: Sources (16)]. - Compare NAT/UDP session timeout on the Basel firewall/NAT policy with other locations / architecture spec (documented finding) [Data: Sources (16)]. - Adjust session timer/NAT session timeout to the value used in working sites (documented: change from 30s to 180s in example) and/or adjust SBC session parameters; validate with test calls [Data: Sources (16)]. 4) Teams notifications (no pop-ups on laptop; phone OK) Documented symptoms and diagnostics: - Messages are delivered but banner/pop-up notifications not shown on laptop; phones OK. Windows Update ran recently and at least one user had Focus Assist active. Remote check found Teams banner notifications disabled plus Windows Focus Assist active [Data: Sources (19)]. Documented remediation steps that led to closure in the prior case: - Per the "Blueprint - Teams Notifications Standard", checks and changes were performed: re-enable Teams banner notifications, disable Focus Assist (or adjust Windows notification center), restart Teams. After settings were adjusted per blueprint, pop-ups returned and ticket was closed [Data: Sources (19)]. Concrete steps based on the record: - Verify Teams notification type for affected users (Banner enabled) and enable it if disabled [Data: Sources (19)]. - Check Windows Notification Center and disable Focus Assist or adjust its rules so Teams banners are allowed [Data: Sources (19)]. - Restart Teams and confirm notifications appear; final verification with user [Data: Sources (19)]. Ownership / support level classification (what the records show) - D365 Financial Reports: handled by System Engineer and required server-level changes and/or Microsoft support escalation in the documented case - this aligns with 2nd-level / specialist support (system engineer / vendor escalation) [Data: Sources (1, 0)]. - New hotline numbers reaching edge but not users: required SBC translation/routing changes and voice architecture adjustments - handled by voice/telephony specialists / infrastructure engineering - 2nd-level / specialist support [Data: Sources (8)]. - Basel calls dropping after ~30s: required SBC/session timer and network NAT timeout changes coordinated with the network team - 2nd-level / specialist support [Data: Sources (16)]. - Teams notifications: handled and resolved by Helpdesk using the standard "Blueprint - Teams Notifications Standard" checks - 1st-level support is appropriate for this symptom [Data: Sources (19)]. Are these connected? - The indexed records show each of these problems had independent root causes and separate remediation activities (AOS/cipher suites, SBC routing, NAT/session timers, Teams/Focus Assist). The historical tickets treated them separately; nothing in the records documents a single cause connecting all four issues. Therefore, based on the indexed evidence, they should be treated as multiple separate incidents, routed to different teams [Data: Sources (0, 1, 8, 16, 19)]. Recommended immediate actions (these are recommendations - not literal extracts from the records) - Open separate tickets (or sub-tickets) for each symptom and assign to the teams indicated above: - D365 FO Financial Reports -> System Engineer / Application/Infrastructure team (2nd-level). Include recent AOS responsiveness metrics, FRDB/Data Mart reset history, and screenshots/logs. (Recommendation) - Hotline new numbers -> Voice/SBC team (2nd-level). Provide carrier trace, SBC logs for the affected numbers, and the new number ranges. (Recommendation) - Basel call drops -> Network + SBC team (2nd-level). Provide SBC session logs, firewall/NAT policy details for the Basel Voice VLAN including UDP session timeout. (Recommendation) - Teams notification pop-ups -> Helpdesk (1st-level). Ask affected laptop users to run the standard checks (Teams banner setting, Windows Notification Center, Focus Assist) and report results; apply blueprint steps if necessary. (Recommendation) If you want, I can: - Produce four concise ticket templates (one per issue) you can paste into your IT system, including required logs and the exact diagnostic checks that were performed/required in the prior cases (recommended next-action text). (Recommendation) If you need copies of the prior case references - D365 Financial Reports incident and resolution (Data Mart Reset, FRDB recreation, IISCRYPTO + AOS restart and Microsoft ticket): [Data: Sources (1, 0)]. - Hotline new number range / SBC translation missing -> routing and translation rules adjusted: [Data: Sources (8)]. - Basel 30s disconnects -> session timer and NAT/UDP timeout mismatch resolved by adjusting NAT timeout and SBC session params: [Data: Sources (16)]. - Teams notifications blocked by Teams banner setting and Windows Focus Assist -> standard blueprint checks and resolution: [Data: Sources (19)]. If any of the incidents need full runbooks or the exact configuration values used in prior fixes, say so. Note: the records contain the actions performed and the high-level parameters changed (e.g., enabling best-practice cipher suites, adjusting NAT/UDP timeout and SBC session timers, updating SBC translation rules, re-enabling Teams banners / disabling Focus Assist) but do not include low-level, site-specific configuration commands or the exact firewall/SBC configuration lines in the indexed text. If you want those exact configuration items, you will need to request them from the infrastructure/voice teams or pull the configuration backups - I do not have further low-level commands in the indexed records.

question_text:
Hi IT, I need help because we have multiple problems at once and people are losing it.

In Zurich (training room), people are trying to make outside calls in Teams. The call goes through and the system shows it's active, the person on the other end can hear us fine, but on our end there's just nothing coming back — total dead air, like our side has no incoming sound at all. We already tried swapping out audio devices entirely (different mic, different speakers) and it makes no difference. Calls between people inside the company work normally.

At the same time in Basel the office is basically unable to print. One new colleague literally has no printers showing up at all on their laptop even though everyone else in the same room can print fine. Another colleague can see the printer in the list, but the moment they try to send something to it they instantly get an "access denied" message and nothing even shows up anywhere. And for the rest of us: documents go into the queue but then just sit there forever like nothing is processing.

We did the usual stuff (restart laptop, unplug dock, change USB port, try a different app) but nothing changed. Does this look connected or just bad timing, and please confirm: should this be classified as a 1st-level or 2nd-level support ticket?
answer: Summary (what the records show) - The Zurich "silent on external Teams calls" scenario in prior incidents was caused by a firewall rule that restricted outbound media (RTP) ports while the location used Media Bypass. Allowing the full media port range per the Direct Routing architecture immediately restored two‑way audio; the fix was implemented by the Unified Communications / Network team and the ticket was handled at System Engineer level and above [Data: Sources (5, 23)]. - Basel printing incidents in the record set were resolved by three different root‑cause actions depending on the symptom: (a) missing network routing/allowance between print server and printer VLAN - adjusted by the Network team so the print server could reach the printers [Data: Sources (22)]; (b) an explicit "Deny" ACE on a printer queue for one user - removed on the print server so the user could print again [Data: Sources (3)]; and (c) a paused printer queue - reactivated and jobs restarted to clear blocked jobs [Data: Sources (27)]. Interpretation / ticket classification (based on the records) - The Teams external-silence issue required Unified Communications and Network team involvement and was worked by System Engineers (2nd‑level) in the documented case [Data: Sources (5, 23)]. - The Basel printing problems in the records were resolved by print‑server and network changes (print server/queue permissions, network routing/firewall rules) - these actions were performed by System Engineers / Network teams (2nd‑level). Individual simple fixes (e.g., unpausing a queue or removing an explicit Deny) were done by Service/Run teams but still on server side [Data: Sources (22, 3, 27)]. - Conclusion: per the documented precedent, treat both problems as 2nd‑level (escalate to Unified Communications + Network for Teams; Print/Server + Network for printing) rather than pure 1st‑level end‑user support [Data: Sources (5, 23, 22, 3, 27)]. Step‑by‑step resolution plan (items explicitly supported by the records) A. Zurich - "external Teams calls: other party can hear us but we hear nothing" 1. Confirm scope (who/where/only external?) - do the same quick checks used in the prior incident: verify it is only external PSTN calls, which locations/users are affected, and whether internal Teams calls work [Data: Sources (5, 23)]. 2. Reproduce and basic client checks (first‑line steps that were performed in the record): perform an internal Teams call (should work), perform an external PSTN test call, clear Teams cache / reinstall client and verify drivers (these were attempted and did not resolve the historic case) [Data: Sources (5, 23)]. 3. Capture signaling and media diagnostics: check Teams Admin Center / SBC logs to confirm SIP signaling and whether RTP is bidirectional. In the documented case SIP/SDP were OK but RTP from clients was not consistently visible to the SBC [Data: Sources (5, 23)]. 4. Check firewall rules for media ports and Media Bypass behavior: compare the Zurich firewall rules to the Direct Routing / media flow specification. The historic failure was that the firewall allowed only STUN/TURN (UDP 3478-3481) but not the dynamic RTP port range, while the location used Media Bypass (so client->SBC RTP must be allowed) [Data: Sources (23)]. 5. Test by temporarily opening the wider UDP port range for media (temporary test): in the record a temporary opening of the wider UDP RTP port range restored audio immediately - use this as a diagnostic step to prove the firewall is the cause [Data: Sources (5, 23)]. 6. Implement permanent firewall change per the Direct Routing architecture (apply documented media port ranges and location‑specific rules). After implementation the team monitored and confirmed stable two‑way audio and closed the ticket [Data: Sources (5, 23)]. B. Basel - multiple printing symptoms (printer unreachable / no printers visible for one user / access denied / jobs stuck) 1. Triage by symptom and follow the prior incident actions: - If print jobs appear in the queue but nothing prints: check the print server queue state (Paused?), check Print Spooler service, and try printing directly from the print server to the printer. In a recorded case the queue was paused and reactivating it plus restarting jobs fixed the issue [Data: Sources (27)]. - If printers are not reachable from the print server (print jobs appear then disappear, server cannot ping the printer): verify network reachability between the print server and the printer. In the Basel case a new device VLAN had been created and the print server lacked routing/allowance to the printer VLAN; adjusting the network rule restored function [Data: Sources (22)]. - If a single user gets "Access denied" while colleagues can print: check the printer queue permissions on the print server for explicit Deny entries and AD group membership. In the recorded single‑user case the user had an explicit Deny ACE on the queue which was removed and printing was restored [Data: Sources (3)]. 2. Apply the fixes that matched the documented root causes: - For network segmentation: coordinate with Network team to open routing/firewall rules between print server and printer VLAN per the "Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard" (this was the resolution in the record) [Data: Sources (22)]. - For per‑user permission errors: on the print server remove the explicit Deny and ensure permissions are assigned via the AD group according to the Print & Scanning standard [Data: Sources (3)]. - For paused queues: reactivate the queue and restart jobs [Data: Sources (27)]. 3. After changes, run test prints from server and multiple user workstations and monitor for successful completion; in the records these tests validated the fixes before ticket closure [Data: Sources (22, 3, 27)]. Actions to assign / teams to involve (as in the records) - Teams silence (Zurich): escalate to Unified Communications (Direct Routing / SBC team) and Network team / firewall admins - these were the teams involved and effected the fix in the documented case [Data: Sources (5, 23)]. - Printing (Basel): involve Print/Server team (to inspect queues and permissions) and Network team (if printers are unreachable due to VLAN/routing) - both roles were required across the documented Basel tickets [Data: Sources (22, 3, 27)]. Items not fully covered by the indexed records (I cannot prove these from the records; these are recommendations) - The record set does not explicitly document a step‑by‑step procedure for the exact symptom "a new colleague has no printers showing up at all while others in same room can print." The closest relevant records show (a) network reachability issues between print server and printer VLAN and (b) per‑user permission deny cases [Data: Sources (22, 3)]. These do not fully cover all causes of "no printers mapped on one client" (GPO/printer deployment issues, client‑side driver mapping, or AD‑group membership errors may also be involved). - Recommended next checks (recommended, not directly documented in the indexed incidents): - Confirm AD group membership and that whatever printer deployment mechanism you use (GPO / print management / SCCM) targeted that new colleague. - On the client: verify printer list after logging off/on, and test mapping the network printer manually (if permitted). - If the client sees no printers at all but others can: capture client event logs for print/subsystem errors and check Group Policy application status (gpresult) to confirm printer policies were applied. - These are recommendations only because the records do not document this exact "no printers visible for new colleague" scenario and its troubleshooting steps. Suggested immediate next steps you can perform now (mix of documented steps and recommended checks; I label which are supported by records) - For Zurich Teams audio (documented): Escalate to Unified Communications and Network, reproduce an external call while collecting SBC/Teams Admin logs, and have Network temporarily open the wider UDP RTP port range to prove/resolve the issue; then implement the architectural firewall change per Direct Routing (allow dynamic RTP range for Media Bypass) and monitor [supported by records: Sources (5, 23)]. - For Basel printing (documented): - Check print server queue status (Paused?) and spooler service, and unpause / restart jobs if needed [supported by records: (27)]. - If jobs disappear or printer unreachable, have Network verify routing/firewall between print server and printer VLAN; apply allowance per Print & Scanning Infrastructure Standard [supported by records: (22)]. - If an individual gets Access Denied, inspect printer queue ACEs on the print server and remove explicit Deny entries and validate AD group membership per the Print & Scanning standard [supported by records: (3)]. - For the new colleague with no printers visible (recommended - not explicitly in the records): check printer deployment (GPO/SCCM), AD group membership, and client GP application status and drivers. If those checks are inconclusive, escalate to Print/Server team for targeted investigation (this escalation path is consistent with who handled similar printing incidents in the records) [related records: (22, 3) but these specific steps are recommendations]. If you want, I can: - Prepare an exact escalation packet to Unified Communications + Network (for Zurich) and to Print/Server + Network (for Basel) containing the reproductions, times, and sample logs to accelerate their work (this would be a recommended action; the records show escalation to those teams worked previously) [Data: Sources (5, 23, 22, 3, 27)]. If any of the above steps should be executed now by your helpdesk, tell me which one(s) and I will draft the exact information to hand to the respective 2nd‑level teams.

question_text:
Hi IT, we need help asap because it feels like the whole company is falling apart since the weekend changes. I’m trying to describe it without guessing, but it’s a mess.

1) In D365 (F&O) the Financial Reports / Report Designer thing is basically broken. It either loads forever or errors out. What’s super strange: older reports suddenly show the author as "unknown" and sometimes it says a report "cannot be found" even when we used it last week. This happens in both test and prod, so we can’t even use test as a workaround.

2) Sales: the desktop mail app is acting up. People can receive emails, but anything they send just sits in Outbox and later fails with a connection-style error. If they send the same email from the browser mailbox it goes through. Mobile seems fine too.

3) External partners: in Teams they are shown as Offline all day even while they are actively writing to us. Chats arrive late and sometimes calls to them do not ring. Internals are fine.

4) Zurich site: external calls in Teams connect, the other side says they hear us, but on our side it’s dead silent (zero audio). We swapped headsets and speakers and it’s the same. Internal calls are ok.

5) Basel site: external calls connect and then drop after about half a minute almost every time, like a timer. Internal calls are ok.

6) Also with the new marketing hotline numbers: calls to the old main number work, but calls to the new number range fail instantly. The provider claims they deliver the call to our edge.

7) Printing is chaos too, but it’s not one single thing. A new employee in Basel sees literally no printers at all, even though everyone next to them can print. Another user can see the printer but gets "Access denied" immediately when printing. And for the rest of us: jobs show up in the queue and then just sit there forever like nothing is processing.

8) One more: in Basel the main office printer sometimes shows jobs for a second and then they vanish, while other printers in the building still work.

People keep blaming laptops, docks, and Windows updates, but all of this started at basically the same time after the weekend work. Is this one connected change or just unlucky timing? And please confirm: should this be classified as a 1st-level or 2nd-level support ticket?
answer: Summary of what's documented in our records vs what is not, and a step‑by‑step resolution path using only documented fixes and diagnostics. Important: below I separate (A) actions and root causes that the indexed tickets actually document, and (B) recommended next steps where the indexed knowledge does not contain a complete resolution. I cite the source ticket(s) for each documented item. 1) D365 F&O - Financial Reports / Report Designer (loads forever, authors shown as "unknown", "report cannot be found") - What the records explicitly document: A nearly identical incident was logged as KF11: Management Report not working (TI-722511).
